one_paragraph_verdict: The article is a standard news report on China's industrial policy, focusing on GDP growth projections and the expansion of computing infrastructure. It fails all five relevance gates. While it mentions state investment and US-China competition, it does so at the level of macroeconomic 'growth engines' and geopolitical rivalry, rather than engaging with the specific theoretical mechanisms of the techno-feudalism debate. It provides no analysis of rent versus profit, the nature of surplus extraction, or the constitutive role of the state in the mode of production as defined in the theory map.
what_article_cannot_adjudicate: Whether AI investments represent productive capital formation or the creation of rent-extracting moats (C1)
The source of AI profitability (indexing vs. data extraction) (C2)
Whether the Chinese state's role in computing infrastructure is constitutive of the mode of production or merely supportive of growth (C3)
Whether AI-driven firms operate under the logic of profit equalization or persistent monopoly rent (C4)
The ratio of tangible to intangible assets in AI firms (C5)
Whether AI extraction practices represent a qualitative break from capitalist expropriation (C6)
state_capital_nexus_relevance: The article mentions state-led infrastructure and US export controls, but these are framed as national security and GDP growth strategies, not as an analysis of the state's constitutive role in the accumulation logic of tech firms.
